title: GPU VRAM Guide
summary: Understand whether GPU acceleration is required, and if so, how model size, batch size, per-sample VRAM, concurrent jobs, and overhead affect memory capacity.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates whether the GPU workload requires acceleration and whether available VRAM is likely to fit the modeled job profile.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it for AI inference, video analytics, machine learning experiments, rendering workloads, GPU-assisted processing, or any compute task where memory on the GPU may be the first limit.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible GPU VRAM Estimator inputs and explain how each value affects acceleration and memory pressure.

items.label: GPU Required?
items.description: Whether the workload actually needs GPU acceleration or can remain CPU-only for the planned design.
items.whyItMatters: Not every compute workload benefits from a GPU. This input keeps the design from adding complexity when acceleration is not part of the requirement.
items.commonMistake: Adding GPU hardware because it sounds powerful without confirming the workload needs it.

items.label: Model Size (GB)
items.description: The base GPU memory footprint of the model, scene, analytics pipeline, or accelerated workload.
items.whyItMatters: The model consumes VRAM before live samples, streams, or batches are added.
items.commonMistake: Only checking GPU compute performance and ignoring memory footprint.

items.label: Batch Size
items.description: The number of samples processed together in one job or execution batch.
items.whyItMatters: Batch size can raise VRAM demand quickly even when the model itself fits.
items.commonMistake: Increasing batch size to improve throughput without checking memory headroom.

items.label: VRAM per Sample (MB)
items.description: The additional GPU memory consumed by each sample, frame, image, stream, or unit of work in the batch.
items.whyItMatters: This value multiplies with batch size and concurrent jobs, so it can dominate total VRAM demand.
items.commonMistake: Testing with a tiny sample and deploying with larger real inputs.

items.label: Concurrent Jobs
items.description: The number of GPU jobs, streams, processes, or workloads active at the same time.
items.whyItMatters: Concurrent jobs can multiply VRAM demand and make a single-GPU design unstable.
items.commonMistake: Sizing for one job and then running many in production.

items.label: Overhead (%)
items.description: Extra VRAM reserved for drivers, framework overhead, buffers, precision changes, workspace memory, and growth.
items.whyItMatters: GPU workloads often need memory beyond the obvious model and batch math.
items.commonMistake: Running VRAM at the edge and then failing when the framework allocates extra workspace.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the modeled GPU memory requirement appears manageable. Watch means batch, concurrency, or overhead should be reviewed. Risk means the job may fail, spill, downscale, or require a different GPU/profile.
bullets: If GPU Required? is set to No, the tool should not force a GPU design.
VRAM pressure is not the same as GPU compute pressure.
Batch and concurrency assumptions must match the real deployment pattern.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: GPU VRAM belongs later in the Compute flow because acceleration should not be considered until the base CPU, RAM, storage, and density profile is understood.
bullets: Use GPU sizing only when the workload actually benefits from acceleration.
Carry GPU decisions into Power / Thermal planning.
Do not choose a GPU by core count alone; memory capacity can be the ceiling.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
